metadata.title: Ecosystems Retrieval Practice
metadata.summary: A short sequence of questions that revisits food chains, roles in an ecosystem and energy transfer.
metadata.subject: science
metadata.learningObjective: Recall how energy moves through a food chain and distinguish producers, consumers and decomposers.

components.text: Ecosystems: can you retrieve it?
Answer from memory first. Check your response, read the feedback and improve any answer you were unsure about.

components.prompt: What is the original source of energy for most food chains?

content.text: The Sun
The soil
Water
Predators

feedback.correct: Correct. Producers capture light energy from the Sun.
feedback.incorrect: Trace the food chain backwards from a consumer to a plant, then ask where the plant gets its energy.
feedback.explanation: Green plants capture light energy during photosynthesis, so the Sun is the original source for most food chains.

components.prompt: What term describes an organism that makes its own food?

feedback.correct: Yes — it is a producer.
feedback.incorrect: Think about the first living organism in a food chain.
feedback.explanation: A producer makes its own food, usually using light energy through photosynthesis.

components.prompt: Match each organism with its role.

content.text: Grass
Rabbit
Fungus
Producer
Consumer
Decomposer

feedback.correct: All three roles are matched correctly.
feedback.incorrect: Ask whether each organism makes food, eats another organism, or breaks down dead material.
feedback.explanation: Grass produces food, a rabbit consumes plants, and fungi decompose dead material.

components.prompt: Sort each factor into the correct group.

content.text: Temperature
Bacteria
Rainfall
Trees

feedback.correct: Correct — organisms are biotic; physical conditions are abiotic.
feedback.incorrect: Biotic means living. Abiotic factors are non-living conditions in the environment.

components.prompt: Put this food chain in order, beginning with the energy source.

content.text: Hawk
Sunlight
Grass
Mouse

feedback.correct: That sequence shows the direction of energy transfer.
feedback.incorrect: Begin with the Sun, then identify what captures its energy and what eats next.
feedback.explanation: Energy moves from sunlight to grass, then to the mouse and finally to the hawk.

metadata.title: Fraction Equivalence Detective
metadata.summary: A misconception-focused check that asks pupils to compare, explain and represent equivalent fractions.
metadata.subject: mathematics
metadata.learningObjective: Recognise equivalent fractions and explain how multiplying a numerator and denominator by the same number preserves value.

components.text: Are these fractions really equal?
Decide before checking. Use a diagram or multiplication rule if you are unsure, then explain any answer you change.

components.prompt: Which fraction has the same value as 3/6?

feedback.correct: Correct. Dividing both 3 and 6 by 3 gives 1/2.
feedback.incorrect: Try simplifying 3/6 by dividing its numerator and denominator by the same factor.
feedback.explanation: Equivalent fractions name the same quantity. Dividing both parts of 3/6 by 3 gives 1/2.

components.prompt: A pupil changes 2/5 to 6/15. Which operation explains why the fractions are equivalent?

content.text: Add 4 to the numerator and 10 to the denominator
Multiply both numerator and denominator by 3
Multiply only the numerator by 3
Add 3 to both numbers

feedback.correct: Exactly. Multiplying both parts by 3 preserves the fraction's value.
feedback.incorrect: Check which operation turns 2 into 6 and 5 into 15 in the same way.
feedback.explanation: Multiplying numerator and denominator by the same non-zero number creates an equivalent fraction.

components.prompt: Complete the statement: 4/6 = 2/3 because both 4 and 6 were divided by ___.
components.placeholder: Enter a number or number word

feedback.correct: Yes. Dividing both parts by 2 simplifies 4/6 to 2/3.
feedback.incorrect: Which common factor changes 4 to 2 and 6 to 3?
feedback.explanation: The common factor is 2, so dividing numerator and denominator by 2 preserves the value.

metadata.title: Persuasive Language Lab
metadata.summary: A compact English-language activity for identifying rhetorical choices and judging their likely effect on an audience.
metadata.subject: english
metadata.learningObjective: Identify persuasive language choices and explain how evidence, rhetorical questions and inclusive language shape a reader's response.

components.text: How is the writer persuading us?
Our neighbourhood throws away hundreds of reusable containers each week. If we bring our own, we can cut waste together. Why wait for someone else to act?
Read the short appeal twice. Name the technique, then connect it to a likely effect rather than merely spotting a phrase.

components.prompt: What is the main effect of the words ‘we’ and ‘together’?

content.text: They create a sense of shared responsibility
They make the appeal sound more technical
They show that the writer is uncertain
They describe an event in the past

feedback.correct: Yes. Inclusive language places the writer and reader on the same side.
feedback.incorrect: Ask how the reader is positioned when the writer says ‘we’ instead of ‘you’.
feedback.explanation: Inclusive pronouns create solidarity and make the proposed action feel collective rather than accusatory.

components.prompt: Why does the writer mention ‘hundreds of reusable containers each week’?

content.text: To give concrete evidence of the scale of the problem
To make the reader laugh
To quote an expert directly
To present the opposing view fairly

feedback.correct: Correct. The quantity makes the waste problem feel specific and substantial.
feedback.incorrect: Consider what the number adds that the vague phrase ‘a lot of waste’ would not.
feedback.explanation: A concrete quantity gives the claim weight, although a critical reader might still ask for the source.

components.prompt: What technique is used in ‘Why wait for someone else to act?’

feedback.correct: Correct — it is a rhetorical question that presses the reader towards action.
feedback.incorrect: The writer asks something without expecting a literal reply.
feedback.explanation: A rhetorical question prompts reflection and implies that delaying action is difficult to justify.

metadata.title: Can We Trust This Source?
metadata.summary: Students evaluate a short historical claim by considering provenance, purpose and corroboration rather than accepting or rejecting it outright.
metadata.subject: humanities
metadata.learningObjective: Evaluate a source's usefulness and reliability by considering who produced it, why it was produced and what other evidence could corroborate it.

components.text: Reliable, useful — or both?
Source A is a campaign leaflet published by a political party before an election. It states: ‘Only our plan can guarantee every family a better future.’
Do not decide from the claim alone. Use the source's origin, intended audience and purpose, then identify evidence that would help you check it.

components.prompt: Which purpose is most likely to have shaped the wording of Source A?

content.text: To win voters' support for the party
To create a neutral record for future historians
To record a private personal reflection
To report the result of a controlled scientific test

feedback.correct: Correct. A campaign leaflet is designed to persuade voters, so its wording is likely to promote the party strongly.
feedback.incorrect: Use both the type of source and the fact that it appeared before an election.
feedback.explanation: Purpose does not make a source useless, but it tells us why its claims and omissions need careful checking.

components.prompt: Which additional evidence would best help a historian test the promise in the leaflet?

content.text: Independent data about family outcomes and detailed records of the proposed plan
A larger printed copy of the same leaflet
A list of colours used in the party's campaign
An anonymous rumour repeated without supporting evidence

feedback.correct: Yes. Independent outcome data and details of the plan let a historian test both the scale and plausibility of the claim.
feedback.incorrect: Choose evidence that adds independent, relevant information rather than repeating the same claim.
feedback.explanation: Corroboration is strongest when evidence is relevant, independently produced and detailed enough to compare with the original claim.

components.prompt: What historical skill means checking a claim against evidence from other sources?

feedback.correct: Correct. Corroboration compares evidence across sources before reaching a judgement.
feedback.incorrect: Think of the term historians use when they look for agreement or disagreement across sources.
feedback.explanation: Corroboration helps a historian test whether claims are supported, qualified or contradicted elsewhere.

metadata.title: Build a Clear Explanation
metadata.summary: Pupils sequence and classify sentences to reveal how a coherent explanatory paragraph is organised.
metadata.subject: english
metadata.learningObjective: Organise an explanatory paragraph using a topic sentence, logically ordered supporting details and a concluding link.

components.text: How does rain form? Build the paragraph.
First sort each sentence by its job. Then put the four sentences in the order that makes the explanation easiest to follow.

components.prompt: Sort each sentence by the job it performs in the paragraph.

content.text: Rain forms through a series of changes in water in the atmosphere.
First, warm water evaporates and rises as water vapour.
Higher in the air, the vapour cools and condenses into droplets.
When the droplets grow heavy enough, they fall as rain and the cycle continues.

categories.label: Supporting detail
categories.description: Explains a stage or gives information

categories.label: Concluding link
categories.description: Completes the explanation and links back

feedback.correct: Correct. The topic frames the explanation, details develop it and the final sentence completes the process.
feedback.incorrect: Look for the broad opening, the sentences that explain individual stages and the sentence that closes the process.
feedback.explanation: A coherent paragraph guides the reader from an overall claim through ordered detail to a meaningful ending.

components.prompt: Now arrange the sentences into the clearest explanatory paragraph.

content.text: When the droplets grow heavy enough, they fall as rain and the cycle continues.
Higher in the air, the vapour cools and condenses into droplets.
Rain forms through a series of changes in water in the atmosphere.
First, warm water evaporates and rises as water vapour.

feedback.correct: Well organised. The explanation moves from the main idea through each stage in time order.
feedback.incorrect: Start with the sentence that introduces the whole process, then follow what happens to the water step by step.
feedback.explanation: The sequence is signalled by ‘First’, the change higher in the air and the final result when droplets become heavy.

metadata.title: Road to Self-Government
metadata.summary: A sequencing and significance activity about major constitutional and political developments in post-war Singapore.
metadata.subject: humanities
metadata.learningObjective: Sequence key developments in Singapore's path to internal self-government and connect each event to its historical significance.

components.text: How did political participation expand?
Arrange the developments by date, then match each one to the change it represents. Dates help with order; significance explains why the sequence matters.

components.prompt: Place the developments in chronological order, earliest first.

content.text: 1957: Lim Yew Hock leads successful constitutional talks
1948: Singapore holds its first limited Legislative Council election
1959: Singapore gains full internal self-government
1955: The Rendel Constitution expands elected representation

feedback.correct: Correct. The sequence shows political participation and local responsibility expanding over time.
feedback.incorrect: Use the dates first, then check that the constitutional changes build towards full internal self-government.
feedback.explanation: Limited elections in 1948 were followed by wider representation in 1955, successful talks in 1957 and internal self-government in 1959.

components.prompt: Match each development with its main significance.

content.text: 1948 election
Rendel Constitution
1957 constitutional talks
1959 internal self-government
Introduced limited electoral participation
Created a legislature with a majority of elected seats
Secured agreement for greater local control
Placed most domestic affairs under an elected local government

feedback.correct: All matched. You have connected chronology to political significance.
feedback.incorrect: Distinguish taking part in elections, expanding elected seats, negotiating change and exercising local control.
feedback.explanation: Each development widened either representation or the responsibilities exercised by locally elected leaders.

metadata.title: Padankan Perkataan di Bilik Darjah
metadata.summary: A bilingual matching and sorting activity for practising common Malay classroom vocabulary in context.
metadata.subject: languages
metadata.learningObjective: Match common Malay classroom nouns and verbs with their English meanings and distinguish actions from objects.

components.text: Apa yang kita buat dan gunakan di kelas?
Padankan setiap perkataan Melayu dengan maksudnya. Kemudian asingkan perkataan kepada perbuatan dan benda.

components.prompt: Padankan perkataan dengan maksud bahasa Inggeris yang betul.

content.text: membaca
menulis
buku
papan putih
reading
writing
book
whiteboard

feedback.correct: Bagus! Semua perkataan dipadankan dengan betul.
feedback.incorrect: Cuba sebut setiap perkataan dalam ayat kelas yang mudah sebelum memadankannya.
feedback.explanation: Membaca means reading, menulis means writing, buku means book and papan putih means whiteboard.

components.prompt: Asingkan perkataan kepada perbuatan dan benda.

content.text: membaca
menulis
buku
papan putih

feedback.correct: Tepat. Membaca dan menulis ialah perbuatan; buku dan papan putih ialah benda.
feedback.incorrect: Tanya: bolehkah saya melakukan perkataan ini, atau bolehkah saya menyentuh bendanya?
feedback.explanation: Awalan meN- sering muncul pada kata kerja seperti membaca dan menulis, manakala buku dan papan putih ialah kata nama.

metadata.title: Bahasa Ikut Situasi
metadata.summary: Pelajar memadankan ungkapan dengan khalayak yang sesuai, kemudian membaiki nada dan susunan sebuah e-mel rasmi.
metadata.subject: languages
metadata.learningObjective: Memilih laras bahasa Melayu yang sesuai dengan khalayak dan tujuan serta menyusun mesej rasmi dengan jelas.

components.text: Cara kita menulis berubah mengikut penerima, tujuan dan keadaan. Mesej kepada rakan boleh mesra dan santai, manakala permohonan kepada guru perlu sopan, lengkap dan jelas.
Padankan setiap petikan dengan situasi komunikasi yang paling sesuai.

components.prompt: Siapakah penerima yang paling sesuai bagi setiap petikan?

content.text: Hai Faris, jom kita ulang kaji selepas sekolah!
Salam sejahtera. Saya ingin memohon kebenaran untuk menggunakan perpustakaan.
Para peserta diminta berkumpul di dewan pada pukul 2 petang.
Terima kasih, Cikgu Aisyah, atas bimbingan yang diberikan.
Mesej santai kepada rakan
E-mel rasmi kepada guru
Pengumuman sekolah
Nota penghargaan kepada guru

feedback.correct: Tepat. Pilihan kata, sapaan dan bentuk ayat sesuai dengan penerima serta tujuan masing-masing.
feedback.incorrect: Perhatikan tahap kesopanan, jenis sapaan dan sama ada petikan itu ditujukan kepada seorang penerima atau sekumpulan orang.
feedback.explanation: Laras bahasa yang berkesan membantu mesej kedengaran wajar dan mudah difahami dalam situasinya.

components.text: Seorang pelajar mahu meminta borang penyertaan daripada gurunya. Draf pertamanya berbunyi: “Kau kena bagi aku borang tu cepat.”

components.prompt: Ayat manakah paling sesuai digunakan dalam e-mel kepada guru?

content.text: Bolehkah cikgu menghantar borang penyertaan tersebut kepada saya sebelum hari Jumaat?
Eh, bagi borang itu sekarang.
Borang itu, kau jangan lupa.
Saya nak borang itu cepat.

feedback.correct: Betul. Ayat itu sopan, khusus dan menyatakan tempoh masa dengan jelas.
feedback.incorrect: Pilih ayat yang menggunakan panggilan hormat, permintaan yang sopan dan maklumat yang khusus.
feedback.explanation: Mesej rasmi tidak perlu berbunga-bunga, tetapi nada, penerima, perkara yang diminta dan tempoh masa harus jelas.

components.text: Susun bahagian e-mel supaya pembaca memahami tujuan dan tindakan yang diminta.

components.prompt: Susun e-mel rasmi ini dari awal hingga akhir.

content.text: Salam sejahtera, Cikgu Farah.
Saya ingin menyertai bengkel penulisan pada cuti sekolah.
Bolehkah cikgu menghantar borang penyertaan kepada saya sebelum hari Jumaat?
Terima kasih atas bantuan cikgu.
Yang benar, Nadia

feedback.correct: Bagus. E-mel itu bergerak daripada sapaan dan tujuan kepada permintaan, penghargaan dan penutup.
feedback.incorrect: Mulakan dengan sapaan dan tujuan, nyatakan permintaan, kemudian akhiri dengan penghargaan serta nama pengirim.
feedback.explanation: Susunan yang teratur membolehkan penerima memahami konteks sebelum membaca tindakan yang diminta.

metadata.title: Market Street Field Notes
metadata.summary: Students separate observations from interpretations, then choose evidence that can support a claim about neighbourhood change.
metadata.subject: humanities
metadata.learningObjective: Distinguish direct observations from interpretations and identify further evidence needed to support a geographical claim.

components.text: A class walks along a fictional Market Street to investigate how the neighbourhood may be changing. Good fieldworkers record what they can directly see or measure before deciding what it might mean.
Sort each note as a direct observation, a cautious interpretation of the observations, or a claim that needs more evidence.

components.prompt: What kind of field note is each statement?

content.text: Four ground-floor units display For Rent signs
Thirty-two people pass the junction in ten minutes
Six new bicycle racks stand beside the bus stop
More residents may be combining cycling with public transport
The whole neighbourhood is becoming unpopular
Most young families have moved away

categories.label: Direct observation
categories.description: Seen, counted or measured during the visit

categories.label: Cautious interpretation
categories.description: A possible meaning suggested by evidence

categories.label: Needs more evidence
categories.description: A broad claim the notes cannot yet support

feedback.correct: Careful work. The observations are specific and checkable, while the wider claims need additional evidence.
feedback.incorrect: Ask whether the note was directly seen or measured, cautiously inferred, or claimed without enough support.
feedback.explanation: Fieldwork becomes more trustworthy when observations are kept separate from interpretations and broad conclusions.

components.text: The class notices twelve recently planted shade trees and records a pavement temperature of 30°C beneath them. They suggest that the trees have made the street cooler in the afternoon.

components.prompt: Which additional evidence would best test the class's claim?

content.text: Temperature measurements from the same places, times and similar weather before and after the trees were planted
One attractive photograph of the new trees
A survey asking pupils which tree they like best
A count of the shops on a different street

feedback.correct: Yes. Matched measurements make the before-and-after temperature comparison much more meaningful.
feedback.incorrect: Choose evidence that measures temperature while keeping place, time and weather as similar as possible.
feedback.explanation: A fair comparison helps separate the possible effect of the trees from differences in weather, time or location.

components.text: Build a responsible fieldwork conclusion that shows what is known, what it might mean and what should be checked next.

components.prompt: Arrange the conclusion in a clear evidence-to-inference order.

content.text: State the specific observations and measurements
Offer a cautious interpretation using words such as may or suggests
Acknowledge what the current evidence cannot prove
Name the additional evidence needed to test the interpretation

feedback.correct: That conclusion is transparent about both the evidence and its limits.
feedback.incorrect: Begin with what was recorded, interpret it cautiously, then identify limitations and the next useful evidence.
feedback.explanation: Responsible geographical reasoning does not hide the gap between a direct observation and a wider claim.

metadata.title: Cool Box Fair-Test Lab
metadata.summary: Students classify variables, diagnose a flawed comparison and rebuild a fair insulation test.
metadata.subject: science
metadata.learningObjective: Identify independent, dependent and controlled variables, then improve a method so that it tests one factor fairly.

components.text: A class wants to find which lining material keeps chilled water coolest. Each team will wrap an identical bottle, wait twenty minutes and measure the temperature increase.
Sort each part of the plan by the job it does in the investigation.

components.prompt: Which factor is changed, measured or kept the same?

content.text: The lining material around each bottle
The water's temperature increase after twenty minutes
The volume of water in each bottle
The starting temperature of the water
The bottle size and material
Where the bottles are placed during the test

feedback.correct: Well planned. One factor changes, one outcome is measured and the other conditions stay consistent.
feedback.incorrect: Ask whether each factor is deliberately changed, observed as the result or held constant.
feedback.explanation: Changing only the lining material helps connect any difference in temperature rise to that material rather than another condition.

components.text: Ravi wraps one bottle in cork and leaves it on a sunny windowsill at 11 am. He wraps another bottle in felt and places it in an air-conditioned storeroom at 2 pm. He then compares their temperature increases.

components.prompt: Which differences make it impossible to tell whether cork or felt is the better insulator? Select both.

content.text: The bottles are tested in different locations
The bottles are tested at different times
Temperature increase is measured for both bottles
The lining materials are different

feedback.correct: Correct. Sunlight, air conditioning and time-of-day conditions could change the result independently of the lining.
feedback.incorrect: The lining must change and the same outcome should be measured. Look for other conditions that changed as well.
feedback.explanation: Both bottles need to experience the same surroundings at the same time so the lining material is the only planned difference.

components.text: Now put the repaired method in an order that gives a fair comparison.

components.prompt: Arrange the method from preparation to conclusion.

content.text: Choose identical bottles and label them cork and felt
Add equal volumes of water at the same starting temperature
Wrap each bottle with the same thickness of its labelled lining
Place both bottles side by side for twenty minutes
Measure and compare each bottle's temperature increase

feedback.correct: That method changes only the lining and makes the temperature comparison meaningful.
feedback.incorrect: Prepare equivalent bottles first, apply the one planned difference, expose them together, then measure the result.
feedback.explanation: A fair sequence establishes the controlled conditions before the test and measures the dependent variable only after equal exposure.

metadata.title: Projectile Motion Explorer
metadata.summary: Change launch conditions, predict the trajectory and connect the graph to the underlying physics.
metadata.subject: science
metadata.learningObjective: Explain how launch speed, angle and gravitational field strength affect a projectile’s path and range.

components.text: How far will it travel?
Predict what will happen before moving one control. Then compare the new curve with your prediction. Air resistance is ignored and launch height is zero.

components.title: Projectile path
components.description: The horizontal axis shows distance from the launch point; the vertical axis shows height.

components.prompt: With angle and gravity unchanged, what happens to the range when launch speed is doubled?

content.text: It stays the same
It doubles
It becomes four times as large
It halves

feedback.correct: Exactly — range is proportional to the square of launch speed in this model.
feedback.incorrect: Try comparing the range readout at 10 m/s and 20 m/s while keeping the other controls fixed.
feedback.explanation: Doubling speed multiplies speed squared by four, so the range becomes four times as large.

metadata.title: Linear Function Explorer
metadata.summary: Students vary gradient and intercept, predict the change and connect the equation y = mx + c with its graph.
metadata.subject: mathematics
metadata.learningObjective: Explain how the gradient and vertical intercept in y = mx + c affect the position and direction of a straight-line graph.

components.text: What does each number do to the line?
Change only one control at a time. Predict whether the line will turn, shift or stay unchanged before moving the slider.

components.title: Graph of y = mx + c
components.description: The horizontal axis is x and the vertical axis is y. The displayed line updates when m or c changes.

components.prompt: What must be true when the line falls from left to right?

content.text: The gradient is negative
The gradient is positive
The intercept is zero
The intercept is positive

feedback.correct: Correct. A negative gradient means y decreases as x increases.
feedback.incorrect: Try a positive and negative value of m while leaving c unchanged. Watch the direction of the line.
feedback.explanation: The sign of m controls direction. The value of c moves the line vertically but does not decide whether it rises or falls.

metadata.title: Rectangles with the Same Perimeter
metadata.summary: Pupils change a target perimeter and explore the possible length-width pairs on a graph.
metadata.subject: mathematics
metadata.learningObjective: Generate different rectangles with the same perimeter and reason about the relationship between width and length.

components.text: How many rectangles can share one perimeter?
Choose an even perimeter. The graph shows possible width-length pairs. Pick whole-number points and check that twice the width plus twice the length gives the target.

components.title: Possible width-length pairs
components.description: Each point on the line represents a rectangle whose width plus length is half the chosen perimeter.

components.prompt: Which equation always checks whether width w and length l match a chosen perimeter P?

content.text: w + l = P
2 × w × l = P
w × l = P

feedback.correct: Correct. A rectangle has two widths and two lengths, so its perimeter is twice their sum.
feedback.incorrect: Count both widths and both lengths around the rectangle.
feedback.explanation: The perimeter rule 2w + 2l = P can be factorised as 2 × (w + l) = P for every value of the control.

metadata.title: Catchment Under Pressure
metadata.summary: Students predict flood pressure, then test how rainfall, hard surfaces and drainage capacity interact in a simplified catchment model.
metadata.subject: humanities
metadata.learningObjective: Explain how rainfall intensity, impermeable surface cover and drainage capacity combine to affect surface-water accumulation.

components.text: A neighbourhood drains into one canal. Rain falling on roofs and paved ground reaches the drains quickly, while rain on planted ground is absorbed or delayed. The next page uses a deliberately simplified teaching model, not a real flood forecast.
Choose the combination you expect to create the greatest surface-water accumulation. Commit to a prediction before testing the controls.

components.prompt: Which catchment is under the greatest pressure?

content.text: Gentle rain, little paved ground and high drainage capacity
Heavy rain, extensive paved ground and low drainage capacity
Heavy rain, little paved ground and high drainage capacity
Gentle rain, extensive paved ground and high drainage capacity

feedback.correct: Good prediction. More rapid runoff enters a system with less capacity to remove it.
feedback.incorrect: Compare both the water entering the drains and the system's capacity to carry it away.
feedback.explanation: Rainfall raises the input, impermeable cover increases the share reaching drains quickly, and drainage capacity removes part of that flow.

components.text: Change one control at a time. In this model, rapid runoff equals rainfall intensity multiplied by the paved share. Surface water accumulates only when that runoff exceeds drainage capacity.

components.title: Accumulated surface water during one hour
components.description: The line starts at zero. Its slope shows how quickly surface water accumulates when rapid runoff is greater than drainage capacity; otherwise it remains at zero.

components.prompt: In this model, when will the plotted line remain flat at zero?

content.text: When drainage capacity is equal to or greater than rapid runoff
Whenever any rain is falling
Whenever impermeable cover is more than half of the catchment
Only during the first thirty minutes

feedback.correct: Correct. The model shows no surface accumulation while the drains can carry away the rapid runoff.
feedback.incorrect: Compare the rapid-runoff readout with the drainage-capacity control.
feedback.explanation: The model clamps negative values to zero: capacity at least as large as rapid runoff gives an accumulation rate of zero.

metadata.title: Classroom Routines Toolkit
metadata.summary: A compact set of ready-made classroom tools for pacing, participation, task tracking and confidence checks.
metadata.subject: other
metadata.learningObjective: Follow a short classroom routine and communicate readiness for the next step.

components.label: Settle-in time
components.completionMessage: Time is up. Face the front and get ready for the next instruction.

components.prompt: Choose how we will discuss the first question.

items.text: Place the required materials on your desk
Prepare one question if anything is unclear

components.title: How ready are you to begin?
components.redLabel: I need help before I begin
